ontology.foodon_label: corn on the cob food product
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for boiled corn on the cob; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium boiled corn cob yields approximately 150 g of edible kernels, providing around 92 kcal, 21.8 g carbohydrate, and 3.0 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled corn, on cob — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Boiled corn, on cob' (record F14070001). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry #169998 (Corn, sweet, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt) for secondary validation of macro plausibility. HPB values are used as primary source per source priority rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Sweet corn, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 52 (low) for boiled sweet corn is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson FS et al. (2021) international GI tables. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (52 × 14.51 × 1.5) / 100 = 11.3 g; the input record states 7.5 which corresponds to a 100 g reference portion GL = (52 × 14.51) / 100 = 7.5. GL per serving retained as 7.5 per 100 g reference portion for consistency with HPB source record.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 156 mg/100 g (borderline range); potassium 218 mg/100 g provides partial offset
hypertension.guidance: Consume boiled corn on the cob without added salt; the 156 mg/100 g sodium content is already in the borderline range, and limiting portion to one medium cob (~150 g edible) keeps total sodium intake manageable while the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (~1.4:1) supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 52 (low), but carbohydrate 14.5 g/100 g with only 1.82 g/100 g dietary fibre; glycaemic load rises with portion size
type2_diabetes.guidance: Limit to half a medium cob (~75 g) per meal and pair with a protein-rich food or non-starchy vegetables to blunt post-meal glucose rise; monitor individual blood glucose response as corn GI can vary between 47 and 60 depending on variety and cooking duration.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and 1.82 g/100 g dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled corn on the cob is a heart-friendly starchy vegetable; its negligible saturated fat and absence of trans fat and cholesterol make it suitable for a cardioprotective diet, provided it is prepared without butter or salt and consumed as part of a fibre-rich, varied meal pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Provides vitamin C (~6.2 mg/100 g) and carotenoid antioxidants, but lacks omega-3 fatty acids and has insufficient fibre (1.82 g/100 g) to qualify as a strong anti-inflammatory food
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled corn on the cob is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis but does not provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit; avoid adding butter (saturated fat) or salt (may worsen tissue swelling) and prioritise omega-3-rich foods and cruciferous vegetables in the same meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled corn on the cob (Zea mays var. saccharata) is a minimally processed starchy vegetable prepared by submerging a whole, husk-removed ear of sweet corn in boiling water for 8–12 minutes until the kernels are tender. The result is a mildly sweet, slightly chewy, and juicy food with a characteristic golden-yellow colour. Per 100 g of edible kernels, it provides 61 kcal, 14.5 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g protein, 0.48 g fat, 1.82 g dietary fibre, and 156 mg sodium. The glycemic index is 52 (low category per University of Sydney GI Database), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 7.5 per 100 g reference portion. Nutritionally, it contributes B-vitamins (thiamine 0.18 mg, niacin 1.77 mg, folate 42 mcg), potassium (218 mg), phosphorus (89 mg), magnesium (26 mg), and vitamin C (6.2 mg). It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (156 mg/100 g) warrants caution for hypertensive individuals; the moderate carbohydrate load requires portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes; and the negligible saturated fat and absence of trans fat and cholesterol make it suitable for cardiovascular health. Boiled corn on the cob is widely consumed across Singapore, Southeast Asia, the Americas, and globally as a street food, side dish, or snack.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F14070001, confidence 0.95), which is the designated primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI value of 52 corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Secondary cross-validation performed against USDA FoodData Central #169998 confirms macro plausibility.
notes: 1. Sodium value of 156 mg/100 g is notably higher than USDA FoodData Central value (~1 mg/100 g for unsalted boiled corn), suggesting the HPB entry reflects corn boiled with added salt or a salted preparation common in Singapore; this is preserved as the canonical HPB value per source priority rules and flagged for downstream user awareness. 2. Saturated fat (0.07 g), monounsaturated fat (0.13 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.22 g), and trans fat (0.0 g) values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central #169998 as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; these are documented as adapted values. 3. Potassium (218 mg), vitamin C (6.2 mg), thiamine (0.18 mg), riboflavin (0.06 mg), niacin (1.77 mg), vitamin B6 (0.09 mg), folate (42 mcg), phosphorus (89 mg), magnesium (26 mg), and zinc (0.46 mg) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central #169998 as secondary reference where HPB does not report these micronutrients. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed values for these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and USDA FoodData Central reports variable or trace values for yellow sweet corn that are not directly transferable to the Singapore boiled preparation. 5. GL per serving of 7.5 is expressed per 100 g reference portion as per the HPB source record; for a 150 g serving the GL is approximately 11.3. 6. No ontology code invented; FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches for boiled sweet corn. SNOMED CT gap noted. 7. This food is not a Singaporean dish by origin; multilingual naming beyond English is not mandatory per G6 rules. All aliases_by_language non-English arrays are empty as no local-language names are required or confidently established for this generic preparation.
